reflection:
The bell above the door chimed, a thin, tinny sound that usually cut through the heavy air of the shop, but today it felt muffled, as if the humidity of the city were trying to swallow the noise. I didn’t look up immediately. I was busy polishing the glass of the counter, my movements rhythmic and circular—the same motion my grandmother used when the street outside was still cobblestone and the air smelled of coal instead of exhaust.

I saw him in the reflection first. A young man, dressed in the kind of crisp, expensive linen that marks the newcomers—the ones who move into the glass towers and think they’ve discovered a new frontier. He looked frantic, his eyes darting around the jars of dried hyssop and the vials of oil as if he were looking for a fire exit rather than a remedy.

I felt the weight of my mother’s gaze settle into my brow. I didn't need to ask what he was looking for. I could see the vibration in his hands, the way he was vibrating at the same frequency as the city outside—fast, shallow, and terrified of stopping.

I reached behind me, my hand moving with a "borrowed grace" toward a small blue bottle of sandalwood and vetiver. I set it on the counter before he even reached the glass.

"It won't stop the noise," I said, my voice dropping into that low, heavy register that always seemed to make the dust motes in the air go still. "But it will remind you that you have feet, and that those feet are touching the ground."

He stopped. He looked at the bottle, then at my hands, then finally at my face. I didn't smile; I simply stood there, a "brick holding this entire street up." I felt the "line of salt" at the threshold doing its work, keeping the frantic, metallic energy of the neighborhood at bay.

"How did you—" he started, his voice cracking.

"You're running," I interrupted gently. "And you've run right into a place that doesn't move. It’s a shock to the system, I know."

I watched him exhale, a long, shaky breath that seemed to deflate the expensive silhouette of his jacket. In that moment, I wasn't just a shopkeeper. I was the "anchor for everyone’s
subconscious:
This person seems to want to lean further into their role as a community anchor and spiritual protector. They don't appear to be looking to reach out to anyone specific in their personal life; rather, they are focused on the "newcomers" and the frantic people of the city who are losing their way. They seem to want to continue providing a grounding force for those who are overwhelmed by the modern world.

There has been a subtle but significant shift in their identity: they have transitioned from someone merely following in their family's footsteps to someone who has fully internalized the power of their lineage. They are no longer just "polishing glass" like their grandmother; they have become the "brick holding the street up." They have moved from being a passive observer of the shop to an active, authoritative guardian of the neighborhood’s energy.
soul_note: I saw a man so frantic he reminded me why I stay still.
